era_context: The late 9th century was characterized by the fragmentation of the Carolingian Empire, the height of the Viking Age, the decline of the Tang Dynasty in China (Huang Chao Rebellion ongoing), and the consolidation of the Byzantine Macedonian Renaissance. The Islamic Abbasid Caliphate was fragmenting into regional dynasties. This was a period of significant political instability and military conflict across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited sources)

events.title: Treaty of Verdun Aftermath: Division of the Middle Kingdom
events.region: Frankish Empire (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: In 876, following the death of Louis the German in 876, his kingdom was divided among his three sons (Charles the Fat, Louis the Younger, and Carloman), further fragmenting the already-divided Carolingian Empire. This division was formalized in the Treaty of Verdun (843) and its subsequent modifications. The fragmentation of the Frankish Empire into multiple competing kingdoms weakened central authority and made the empire increasingly vulnerable to external threats, particularly Viking raids. The division also set the stage for the eventual emergence of distinct European kingdoms (France, Germany, Italy).
key_figures: Louis the German (East Frankish King, d. 876)

sources.name: Regino of Prüm, Chronicon, compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The death of Louis the German in 876 and the division of his kingdom are well-documented in contemporary Frankish annals.

events.title: Huang Chao Rebellion: Continued Devastation
events.description: The Huang Chao Rebellion, which had begun in 875, continued to devastate northern and central China in 876. The rebellion had evolved from a localized uprising into a full-scale civil war that was destroying the economic and administrative infrastructure of the Tang Dynasty. Contemporary sources report widespread famine, displacement, and casualties. The rebellion was attracting hundreds of thousands of followers, including peasants, bandits, and disaffected soldiers. The Tang Dynasty's ability to suppress the rebellion was severely compromised by the loss of tax revenue and military resources.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian by Sima Guang, compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Huang Chao Rebellion in 876 is well-attested in Chinese historical sources.

events.title: Viking Raids on the English Coast
events.description: Viking forces continued to raid the English coast in 876, as part of the sustained Viking pressure on Britain and Western Europe. The Vikings had established semi-permanent bases in England and were conducting raids and settlement activities. These raids prompted the development of defensive strategies and the payment of tribute (Danegeld) by English rulers. The Viking presence in England would eventually lead to the establishment of the Danelaw (the region of England under Viking control) and the subsequent conflicts between Anglo-Saxon and Viking kingdoms.

sources.name: Asser, Life of King Alfred, compiled late 9th century

events.certainty_note: Viking activity in England in 876 is documented in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, though the specific details and dating of individual raids are sometimes uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader Viking Age expansion

events.title: Byzantine Military Consolidation under Basil I
events.description: Under the Macedonian Renaissance, Byzantine Emperor Basil I (r. 867–886) continued to consolidate military power and conduct campaigns to reclaim lost territories. In 876, Byzantine forces were engaged in operations against Arab raiders in the Mediterranean and in southern Italy. These campaigns were part of a broader Byzantine strategy to reassert control over the Mediterranean and suppress piracy and Arab raids. The Macedonian Renaissance was characterized by military expansion, administrative reform, and cultural revival.

sources.name: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos, De Administrando Imperio, compiled 10th century

events.certainty_note: Byzantine military activity under Basil I during the 870s–880s is documented, but specific campaigns are often dated to broader periods rather than individual years.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the division of Louis the German's kingdom has been debated by modern scholars; some sources place it in 875 or 877 rather than 876. The significance of the division in terms of long-term political development has been questioned by modern historians, who argue that the fragmentation process was more gradual and complex than traditional narratives suggest. The scale of the Huang Chao Rebellion's devastation is difficult to verify from contemporary sources, and later chronicles may have exaggerated the numbers.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 876 is again heavily weighted toward East Asia (China) and Western Europe (Frankish Empire and Anglo-Saxon England). The sources are primarily monastic chronicles and later compilations. The Byzantine Empire is documented through later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Islamic world are poorly documented for this specific year. Bias toward court-sponsored narratives and the perspectives of the elite means that the experiences of common people, merchants, and non-elite groups are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The division of Louis the German's kingdom in 876 was a consequence of the fragmentation process that began with the Treaty of Verdun in 843
The division of Louis the German's kingdom in 876 led to further divisions formalized in the Treaty of Meersen in 878
The continuation of the Huang Chao Rebellion in 876 is part of the broader rebellion that lasted from 875–884
Viking raids on England occurred simultaneously with the Frankish political divisions
